Clinical Anatomy · Cervical Spine

Cervical Pain Syndromes

Whiplash injury · Cervical radiculopathy · Cervical facet osteoarthritis — epidemiology, presentation & pathophysiology

Compare Whiplash Radiculopathy Facet OA Distinguish Rapid Review
1

Compare at a Glance

Use this table and the cards below to separate the three disorders the moment you see a neck-pain stem.

FeatureWhiplashCervical RadiculopathyFacet OA
Typical patientAny age; after MVCMean age ~48; males more commonAge >50; older adults
MechanismAcceleration–deceleration forceDisc herniation or spondylosisCumulative joint degeneration
Pain characterAxial neck pain & stiffness; may be delayedRadiates into shoulder, arm, or handAxial pain/stiffness, movement-provoked
Neurologic findingsNone / mild paresthesiasDermatomal sensory loss, weakness, diminished reflexesNone / non-specific referred pain
Classic clueMVC + axial pain, no arm neurologyArm/hand symptoms in a root patternOlder patient + stiff neck + no arm neurology
Pathophysiology anchorSoft-tissue + joint injury; mechanism unclearNerve root compression / irritationCartilage erosion, osteophytes, joint-space loss
WhiplashSoft-tissue acceleration–deceleration injury
Epidemiology
16–200 per 100,000; more common in women
Presentation
Neck pain/stiffness (may be delayed), headache, dizziness, paresthesia
Pathophysiology
Unclear; microvascular bleeding + inflammatory mediators; soft-tissue & joint injury
⚡ MVC mechanism + axial pain/stiffness, no clean dermatomal deficit
Cervical RadiculopathyNerve root compression syndrome
Epidemiology
1.2–5.8 per 1,000; mean age 48; higher in males
Presentation
Pain radiating into arm/hand; sensory loss, weakness, or diminished reflex in root pattern
Pathophysiology
Root compression from disc herniation or spondylosis cascade → foraminal narrowing
⚡ Dermatomal arm/hand symptoms ± diminished reflex
Cervical Facet OAZygapophyseal joint degeneration
Epidemiology
Common in >50 yo; C6/C7 most common; risk: age, obesity, prior whiplash
Presentation
Axial pain/stiffness with movement, reduced ROM; referred pain — no true radiculopathy
Pathophysiology
Cartilage erosion, ligament thickening, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis
⚡ Older patient + mechanical neck pain + no upper-extremity neurologic deficit
2

Whiplash Injury

Whiplash

Think: sudden backward head translation → cervical extension then flexion → soft-tissue & joint injury
Epidemiology
  • Global incidence 16–200 per 100,000
  • More common in women (9.6%) than men (7.7%)
  • Most often follows rear-end or side-impact MVCs
Clinical presentation
  • Neck pain and stiffness — immediate or delayed by several days
  • Headache, shoulder/back pain, dizziness
  • Paresthesia, fatigue, sleep disturbances
  • Some patients develop persistent low-intensity pain
Pathophysiology: Described as unclear in source material. Proposed mechanisms include microvascular bleeding and local inflammatory mediator release at the time of injury.
Structures that may be injured
  • Intervertebral joints and discs
  • Ligaments (including anterior longitudinal ligament)
  • Cervical muscles
  • Nerve roots
Specific injury patterns
  • Hyperflexion injuries → upper cervical region
  • Hyperextension injuries → commonly at C5
  • Zygapophyseal joint process fracture, hemarthrosis
  • Joint capsule tears, microtears of the ALL
3

Cervical Radiculopathy

Cervical Radiculopathy

Think: nerve root problem — arm symptoms matter as much as neck pain
Epidemiology
  • Prevalence 1.2–5.8 per 1,000
  • Mean age ~48 years
  • Highest incidence in males and middle-aged adults
Risk factors
  • Smoking
  • Repetitive / increased axial loading of cervical spine
  • History of lumbar radicular problems
  • IV substance use; long-term steroid use
Degenerative cascade: Disc desiccation → decreased disc height → weakened annulus fibrosus → increased joint stress → ligamentous hypertrophy + osteophyte formation → foraminal narrowing → nerve root compression or irritation.
Clinical hallmark
  • Pain radiating from neck into shoulder, arm, or hand
  • Dermatomal sensory loss or paresthesia
  • Root-specific motor weakness
  • Diminished deep tendon reflex corresponding to the affected root

High-Yield Root Patterns

Exam strategy: Identify the root from the reflex first (biceps/brachioradialis = C5–C6; triceps = C7), then confirm with sensory territory and motor weakness.
RootPain / Sensory territoryMotor weaknessReflex affected
C1No major distal limb patternMild neck flexion/extension weakness—
C2Posterior scalp pain, paresthesia, sensory lossMild neck flexion/extension weakness—
C3Back of head, upper neck/ear, possibly jawNeck flexion/extension weakness—
C4Mid/lower neck; lower neck/shoulder sensory lossShoulder elevation weakness—
C5Proximal neck/shoulder; lateral upper arm sensory lossShoulder abduction, elbow flexion weaknessBiceps, brachioradialis ↓
C6Neck → shoulder → lateral forearm → hand; lateral forearm/hand sensory lossShoulder abd/ER, elbow flexion, pronation weaknessBiceps, brachioradialis ↓
C7 ⭐Neck → shoulder → distal hand/middle finger (most common root)Elbow extension, pronation, wrist flexion weaknessTriceps ↓
C8Medial forearm and hand sensory lossWrist and hand weakness—
4

Cervical Facet Osteoarthritis

Cervical Facet OA

Think: degenerative zygapophyseal joint pain — axial and mechanical, not a nerve-root syndrome
Epidemiology
  • Common in the elderly; many adults >50 show degeneration
  • Most common level: C6/C7
Risk factors
  • Age, obesity, poor posture
  • Degenerative disc disease
  • Smoking
  • Prior neck injury / whiplash
Pathophysiology: Not fully understood; cytokines and proteolytic enzymes are suspected mediators. Cartilage erosion and inflammation, ligament thickening, osteophyte formation, and subchondral bone changes with hypomineralization occur over time.
Structural pathology
  • Loss of joint space and articular cartilage
  • Joint-space irregularity
  • Subchondral sclerosis
  • Osteophytes bridging the joint space
Clinical presentation
  • Pain and stiffness with neck movement
  • Spontaneous pain or pain after flexion-extension
  • Referred pain to shoulders, periscapular region, or occiput
  • Decreased cervical ROM; occasional neck spasms
  • Generally does not radiate into the upper extremity
5

How to Distinguish Them Quickly

Use the symptom pattern first, then fit the epidemiology and mechanism around it.

Whiplash — mechanism-heavy
The stem gives you a sudden acceleration–deceleration event (rear-end MVC is classic). The patient develops axial neck pain and stiffness — often delayed by days — with headache and dizziness, but no clean dermatomal neurologic deficit.
⚡ MVC mechanism + axial pain/stiffness, no arm neurology
Radiculopathy — neuro-heavy
The differentiating feature is pain radiating into the arm or hand with sensory, motor, or reflex findings that map to a specific nerve root. Neck pain may be mild or absent; the arm symptoms do the talking.
⚡ Dermatomal arm/hand pain, numbness, or diminished reflex
Facet OA — axial mechanical
Usually an older patient with neck stiffness and movement-provoked pain, reduced cervical ROM, and possibly referred pain to the shoulder or occiput — but no true upper-extremity radicular syndrome.
⚡ Older patient + stiff painful neck + no arm neurologic deficit
Three-question triage: (1) Was there a sudden force? → whiplash. (2) Does pain go into the arm/hand with neurologic signs? → radiculopathy. (3) Older patient with mechanical axial stiffness and no arm neuro? → facet OA.
6

Rapid Review

Click to reveal the answer.

1
What is the classic mechanism of whiplash?▾
Sudden acceleration–deceleration force (typically rear-end or side-impact MVC) causing cervical extension then flexion, injuring soft tissues and joints.
2
Is whiplash pathophysiology fully understood?▾
No — the source material explicitly describes it as unclear. Proposed mechanisms include microvascular bleeding and local inflammatory mediator release.
3
Which levels are associated with hyperflexion vs hyperextension injuries in whiplash?▾
Hyperflexion → upper cervical region. Hyperextension → commonly at C5.
4
What is the prevalence and mean age for cervical radiculopathy?▾
Prevalence 1.2–5.8 per 1,000. Mean age approximately 48 years. Higher incidence in males.
5
Describe the degenerative cascade leading to cervical radiculopathy.▾
Disc desiccation → decreased disc height → weakened annulus → increased joint stress → ligamentous hypertrophy + osteophyte formation → foraminal narrowing → nerve root compression.
6
C7 radiculopathy: pain territory, motor finding, and reflex?▾
Pain: neck → shoulder → distal hand / middle finger. Motor: weak elbow extension, pronation, wrist flexion. Reflex: triceps reflex diminished. C7 is the most commonly affected root.
7
How do you distinguish C5 from C6 radiculopathy?▾
Both affect the biceps/brachioradialis reflex. C5 → lateral upper arm sensory loss + shoulder/elbow weakness. C6 → lateral forearm and hand sensory loss + pronation weakness.
8
What is the most common facet joint level affected by cervical OA?▾
C6/C7.
9
Name three structural changes seen in cervical facet OA.▾
Any three of: joint-space loss, articular cartilage loss, joint-space irregularity, subchondral sclerosis, osteophyte bridging.
10
What is the single best clinical differentiator between radiculopathy and facet OA?▾
Radiculopathy produces dermatomal arm/hand symptoms with neurologic signs (sensory loss, weakness, diminished reflexes). Facet OA produces axial/mechanical neck pain with referred pain to the shoulder or occiput but no true upper-extremity neurologic deficit.
11
Which condition is prior whiplash a known risk factor for?▾
Cervical facet osteoarthritis.
7

Exam-Style MCQs

These are built to force you to separate mechanism-driven pain from root-pattern pain.

1
A patient is rear-ended at a stoplight and develops neck stiffness and headache two days later. The pain is axial, and the neurologic exam of the upper extremities is normal. Which diagnosis best fits?
A. Whiplash injury
B. Cervical radiculopathy
C. Cervical facet osteoarthritis
D. Thoracic outlet syndrome
▾
Answer: A. Whiplash injury. The key clues are the acceleration-deceleration mechanism, delayed onset of axial neck pain and stiffness, and lack of a clean dermatomal neurologic deficit. Cervical radiculopathy would give arm symptoms and focal neurologic findings, while facet OA is typically an older-patient mechanical pain syndrome rather than post-MVC.
2
A patient has pain radiating from the neck into the middle finger, weak elbow extension, and a diminished triceps reflex. Which root is most likely affected?
A. C5
B. C6
C. C7
D. C8
▾
Answer: C. C7. Middle-finger pain with triceps reflex loss is the classic C7 radiculopathy pattern. The associated weakness in elbow extension, pronation, and wrist flexion further supports C7 as the most likely root.
✕
